options.why: Left the fraction unchanged instead of swapping the numerator and denominator.
Gave the opposite (negative) of the number instead of its reciprocal.
Converted $\frac{4}{5}$ to the decimal $0.8$ instead of finding the reciprocal.
Left the whole number unchanged instead of writing it over $1$ and swapping.
Gave the opposite of $7$ instead of its reciprocal.
Assumed every whole number has a reciprocal of $1$.
Swapped only the fraction part, ignoring the whole number, instead of converting first.
Converted to the improper fraction $\frac{11}{4}$ but forgot to swap it.
Kept the whole number $2$ and flipped only the fraction part.

questions.question_text: The reciprocal of a number is $\frac{8}{5}$. What is the number?

options.why: Gave the reciprocal itself instead of the original number.
Gave the opposite of $\frac{8}{5}$ instead of reversing the reciprocal.
Rewrote $\frac{8}{5}$ as the mixed number $1\frac{3}{5}$ instead of finding the original number.

questions.solution_text:
Swapping numerator and denominator is its own reverse.
The number is $\frac{5}{8}$.
Check: $\frac{5}{8}\times\frac{8}{5}=1$.

questions.question_text: Which of these numbers is equal to its own reciprocal?

options.why: Its reciprocal is $\frac{1}{2}$, which is not equal to $2$.
Its reciprocal is $2$, which is not equal to $\frac{1}{2}$.
Zero has no reciprocal, because you cannot divide by $0$.

questions.solution_text:
A number equals its own reciprocal when $n=\frac{1}{n}$.
$n^{2}=1$
$n=1$
